content.full_text:
April 14, 1989


Honorable Frederick A. Jones
Judge of the Superior Court
800 South Victoria Avenue
Ventura, CA 93009

                                          Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                               Our File No. A-89-169

Dear Judge Jones:

     This will confirm our telephone conversation of February
22, 1989, in which we discussed the use of your accumulated
campaign funds in light of the decision in California Common
Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles County Superior Court Case No.
C709383.). Since those funds were acquired prior to January
1, 1989, they would be restricted funds to be used for any
lawful purpose other than to support or oppose a candidate.
(Government Code Section 85306.) Thus, even though these
funds were composed of contributions within statutory limits
and deposited in a campaign bank account for a future
campaign, the funds could not be used for future campaign
expenditures unless the California Common Cause decision were
reversed on appeal. I sent you a copy of Section 18536.2 of
the California Code of Regulations which sets forth
appropriate uses of restricted funds.

     As I explained to you, the California Common Cause case
is still subject to appeal. We have been advising that no
action be taken with respect to the disposition of restricted
funds pending the appeal. If the funds now are placed in a
restricted account, that election probably could not be
reversed in the event that the California Common Cause
decision were reversed on appeal.

     Notwithstanding this advice, you have decided not to
await the outcome of the California Common Cause decision.
You would prefer to close your campaign bank account and use
the accumulated funds to repay campaign loans owed by your
1978 Municipal Court election committee. You have outlined
the steps that you believe would be appropriate to accomplish

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-

Honorable Frederick A. Jones
April 14, 1989
Page 2

this result in your letter of March 14, 1989 (copy enclosed.)
We have reviewed the proposed sequence and agree that it
would be an acceptable way to proceed.

If you have any questions about this letter, I may be
reached at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Margaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:MWE:aa

Enclosure

CHAMBERS OF

The Superior Court
FREDERICK A. JONES
HALL OF JUSTICE
800 SOUTH VICTORIA AVENUE
VENTURA, CA 93009
(805) 654-2915

March 14, 1989

Ms. Margaret Ellison
Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Ellison:

Thank you for your recent advice regarding my apparently somewhat
unique Proposition 73 predicament.

I have reviewed the materials you provided and do now agree
that, given California Common Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles Superior
Court No. C709383), the election campaign money I accumulated
while a judge of the Municipal Court may not continue to be
held or later spent to promote my election as judge of the
Superior Court. From among the available courses we discussed,
I have decided to use the funds to partially repay the still
outstanding loans made in 1978 to my Municipal Court election
committee. This will exhaust all funds on deposit leaving
nothing to be carried over for a future, Superior Court election
campaign even if a change in law permitting same should occur.

We did not discuss the procedural requirements of such a transfer
and disbursement, but it seems to me that the following described
sequence is appropriate:

     1.  Open a bank checking account in the name of my existing
Municipal Court judge election controlled committee for the
sole purpose of receiving funds from the Superior Court re-election
FPPC Form 502 Campaign Bank Account. As I understand the existing
regulations no new FPPC Form 502 relating to the establishment
of this conduit account need be submitted.

     2.  Transfer all money on deposit in the above Form 502
account to the Municipal Court election account and thereby
terminate the Form 502 account. Upon such transfer and deposit,
file an amended FPPC Form 502 formally notifying the FPPC of
such termination.

Ms. Margaret Ellison
March 14, 1989
Page Two


     3.  Upon closure of the FPPC Form 502 bank account, file
with the Secretary of State a Form 415 Statement of Termination
ending the existence of the Superior Court re-election committee
which was formed for the sole purpose of controlling the FPCC
Form 502 Campaign Bank Account.  Also, file a new FPPC Form
501 terminating my earlier announced intention to seek the
office of Superior Court Judge in November 1994.

     4.  From the Municipal Court election committee checking
account repay campaign loans until the funds are exhausted
and then,

     5.  Terminate the Municipal Court election committee and
close the controlled account.

     As I mentioned, I am anxious to comply with existing law as
presently interpreted, exhaust all funds on hand, engage in
no solicitation of campaign funds in the near future, and terminate
all election committees.

     If you conclude any of my intended steps to these ends are
inappropriate or not permitted, please so inform me.

Sincerely,

Frederick A. Jones

FREDERICK A. JONES
Judge of the Superior Court

FAJ:spg

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 22, 1989


Honorable Frederick A. Jones
Judge of the Superior Court
Hall of Justice
800 South Victoria Avenue
Ventura, CA 93009

Re: Letter No. 89-169

Dear Judge Jones:

We received your letter requesting confirmation of advice
under the Political Reform Act on March 20, 1989. Your letter has
been assigned to Margaret Ellison for response. If you have any
questions, you may contact Ms. Ellison directly at (916) 322-5901.

If the letter is appropriate for confirmation without further
analysis, we will attempt to expedite our response. A confirming
response will be released after it has gone through our approval
process. If the letter is not appropriate for this treatment, the
staff person assigned to prepare the response will contact you
shortly to advise you. In such cases, the normal analysis, review
and approval process will be followed.

You should be aware that your letter and our response are
public records which may be disclosed to any interested person
upon receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

June 2, 1989

Ms. Margaret Ellison
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: File #A-89-169

Dear Ms. Ellison:

Enclosed for your information herewith are copies of the Form
410 Statements of Organization submitted to the Secretary of
State, and for the Ventura County Clerk as required to comply
with Proposition 73 campaign fund restrictions.

Thank you for your cooperation in this matter.

Very truly yours,

Frederick A. Jones

FREDERICK A. JONES
Judge of the Superior Court

The purpose of this amendment is to bring subsection VII into
conformity with that Form 410 to be filed with the Secretary of

Thank you for your cooperation in this matter.

Very truly yours,

Frederick A. Jones
FREDERICK A. JONES
Judge of the Superior Court

For information required to be provided to you pursuant to the Information Practices Act of 1977, see "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure
Provisions of the Political Reform Act."

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING COVER PAGE
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION
FORM 410

NAME AND ADDRESS OF COMMITTEE:

Provide the full name of the committee. A committee may use only one name. If this committee is
controlled by an individual, entity or other person, the name of the individual, entity or other person must be
included in the name of the committee. For example, when a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state
ballot measure controls a committee, his or her name must be included in the name of the committee.

There are special rules for "sponsored committees," see Part V.

In the case of a committee supporting or opposing a ballot measure as its principal activity, all
references to the committee must be made by the Political Reform Act and must include the statement,
"Committee For Proposition ________," if supporting a measure, or "Committee Against Proposition
________," if opposing a measure.

The street address of the committee must be reported. A post office box number is NOT acceptable.

The mailing address of the committee must be reported if it is different from the committee's street
address. A post office box number is acceptable for the mailing address.

DATE QUALIFIED AS A COMMITTEE:

Date qualified is the date the committee received contributions totaling $1,000 or more during a
calendar year. If a Form 410 is filed prior to receipt of $1,000 in contributions, enter "Not Yet
Qualified" in the "Date Qualified as Committee" space.

I. TREASURER AND OTHER PRINCIPAL OFFICERS:

The treasurer's name, home or business address, a mailing address, if different than home or business
address, and a phone number where the treasurer can be reached during business hours must be
listed.

If the committee has "principal officers" other than the treasurer, such as a president, secretary or
chairperson, they must be identified.

II. IS THIS A BROAD BASED POLITICAL COMMITTEE?

To qualify as a "broad based committee," the committee may not be controlled by a candidate or
officeholder, the committee must have been in existence for more than six months, must have received
contributions from 100 or more persons at any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year (July 1
- June 30) and the previous two fiscal years, and must have made contributions to five or more candidates at
any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year and the previous two fiscal years.

Enter the date on or before which the committee qualified as a broad based committee. If the committee
qualified as a broad based committee on or before January 1, 1989, enter "On or before January 1, 1989."

III. IS THIS COMMITTEE CONTROLLED BY A CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR PROPONENT
OF A STATE BALLOT MEASURE?

A committee is controlled by a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state ballot measure if the
candidate, officeholder or proponent, their agent or any other committee they control, has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee.

Because of new provisions contained in Proposition 73 which was passed by the voters on June 7, 1988,
candidates and officeholders may not jointly control committees formed to support their own candidacies, or
committees formed to support or oppose other candidates. Candidates and officeholders may jointly control
a committee whose only activity is supporting or opposing a ballot measure.

COMMITTEE: FREDERICK A. JONES, SUPERIOR COURT JUDGE, REELECTION COMMITTEE

V IS THIS A SPONSORED COMMITTEE? (Refer to the instructions on the reverse side for definitions and rules regarding
the name of a sponsored committee.)

☒ NO
☐ YES (Provide name and address of sponsor If the committee has more than one sponsor, provide the name and
address of each sponsor on an attachment.)

Address of Sponsor: ________________________________________________________________________________

CANDIDATES OR MEASURES SUPPORTED OR OPPOSED.

CANDIDATE'S NAME/MEASURE'S FULL TITLE
INCLUDING BALLOT NUMBER OR LETTER SUPPORT OPPOSE OFFICE SOUGHT OR HELD BY CANDIDATE OR MEASURE'S
JURISDICTION (Include district number, city or county, as applicable.)

FREDERICK A. JONES XX SUPERIOR COURT JUDGE

VENTURA COUNTY


Attach additional information on appropriately labeled continuation sheets.

CHECK BOX TO INDICATE THE COMMITTEE'S LEVEL OF ACTIVITY: ☐CITY ☐COUNTY ☐STATE




Attach additional information on appropriately labeled continuation sheets.

ALL FUNDS TO BE TRANSFERRED TO THE CITIZEN COMMITTEE TO ELECT FREDERICK A.

JONES MUNICIPAL COURT JUDGE, ID # 781378


VERIFICATION

I HAVE USED ALL REASONABLE DILIGENCE IN PREPARING THIS STATEMENT. I HAVE REVIEWED THE STATEMENT AND TO THE BEST OF MY KNOWLEDGE THE
INFORMATION CONTAINED HEREIN IS TRUE AND COMPLETE.

I CERTIFY UNDER PENALTY OF PERJURY UNDER THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA THAT THE FOREGOING IS TRUE AND CORRECT.

EXECUTED ON _____________ AT VENTURA, CA ______ BY _________________________________ _________
(DATE) (CITY AND STATE) (SIGNATURE OF TREASURER)

EXECUTED ON _____________ AT VENTURA, CA ______ BY _________________________________
(DATE) (CITY AND STATE) (SIGNATURE OF CONTROLLING CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR STATE MEASURE PROPONENT)

EXECUTED ON _____________ AT _________________________ BY _________________________________
(DATE) (CITY AND STATE) (SIGNATURE OF CONTROLLING CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR STATE MEASURE PROPONENT)

EXECUTED ON _____________ AT _________________________ BY _________________________________
(DATE) (CITY AND STATE) (SIGNATURE OF CONTROLLING CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR STATE MEASURE PROPONENT)

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING PAGE TWO
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION

FORM 410

V SPONSORED COMMITTEE:

"Sponsored committee" means a recipient committee, other than a candidate's or officeholder's controlled committee,
which has one or more sponsors. Most political action committees are "sponsored committees."

An organization, business or other entity is a sponsor if any of the following apply:

(1) The committee receives all or nearly all of its contributions from the entity or organization, or its members, officers,
employees, or shareholders; or

(2) The organization collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll deductions or dues from its
members, officers or employees; or

(3) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organizations or entities, provides all or nearly all of
the administrative services for the committee; or

(4) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organizations, sets the policies for soliciting
contributions or making expenditures of committee funds.

A sponsored committee (this includes most political action committees), must include the name of its sponsor in the name of
the committee. If the sponsored committee has more than one sponsor and the sponsors are members of an industry or other
identifiable group, the name of the committee must include a term identifying that industry or group.

VI IF PRIMARILY FORMED TO SUPPORT OR OPPOSE SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR MEASURES, LIST
SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR MEASURES SUPPORTED OR OPPOSED:

A committee is "primarily formed" when its principal activity is raising or spending money to support or oppose a specific
candidate or measure or a group of specific candidates all being voted upon in the same jurisdiction on the same date. This
type of committee is usually formed for a specific election and then terminates after the election when all debts have been
paid and there are no surplus funds.

VII COMMITTEE'S PRINCIPAL ACTIVITY IF NOT SUPPORTING OR OPPOSING SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR
MEASURES:

A committee which does not support or oppose specific candidates or measures as its primary activity must provide a brief
description of its political activities such as whether or not it supports candidates or measures which share a common political
affiliation.

VIII YOU MUST SPECIFY WHAT DISPOSITION WILL BE MADE OF SURPLUS FUNDS IN THE EVENT OF
TERMINATION

A committee must specify what use will be made of any surplus funds. Elections Code Section 12400, et seq. prohibits
personal use of campaign funds and specifies permissible uses of surplus campaign funds. Elections Code Section 22795
specifies permissible uses of funds received by state ballot measure committees. The Fair Political Practices Commission has no
authority to interpret the Elections Code. Contact the enforcement official for your jurisdiction if you have any questions.

VERIFICATION:

The statement must be signed and verified by the committee treasurer. If this committee is controlled by a candidate,
officeholder or state ballot measure proponent, the candidate, officeholder or proponent must also sign and verify the
statement. If a ballot measure committee is controlled by two or three candidates or officeholders, each candidate must sign
the Statement of Organization. If more than three candidates or officeholders control a ballot measure committee, one of
the candidates or officeholders may sign the Statement of Organization on behalf of all controlling candidates or
officeholders.

CHAMBERS OF

The Superior Court
FREDERICK A. JONES
HALL OF JUSTICE
800 SOUTH VICTORIA AVENUE
VENTURA, CA 93009
(805) 654-2915

June 1, 1989

Secretary of State
Political Reform Division
Post Office Box 1467
Sacramento, California 95812-1467

Gentlemen:

Enclosed is Form 410 pertaining to Frederick A. Jones, Superior
Court Judge, Re-election Committee.

The sole purpose of the establishment of this committee is to
facilitate and document the transfer of already deposited FPPC
Form 502 Campaign Bank Account funds to an account controlled by
my controlled committee I.D. Number 781378.

Thank you for your cooperation in this matter.

Very truly yours,

Frederick A. Jones

FREDERICK A. JONES
Judge of the Superior Court

                   (DATE)           (CITY AND STATE)                        (SIGNATURE OF CONTROLLING CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR STATE MEASURE PROPONENT)

                   (DATE)           (CITY AND STATE)                        (SIGNATURE OF CONTROLLING CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR STATE MEASURE PROPONENT)

                   (DATE)           (CITY AND STATE)                        (SIGNATURE OF CONTROLLING CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR STATE MEASURE PROPONENT)

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING PAGE TWO
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION

FORM 410

V     SPONSORED COMMITTEE:

"Sponsored committee" means a recipient committee, other than a candidate's or officeholder's controlled committee,
which has one or more sponsors. Most political action committees are "sponsored committees."

An organization, business or other entity is a sponsor if any of the following apply:

(1) The committee receives all or nearly all of its contributions from the entity or organization, or its members, officers,
     employees, or shareholders; or

(2) The entity or organization collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll deductions or dues from its
     members, officers or employees; or

(3) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organizations or entities, provides all or nearly all of
     the administrative services for the committee; or

(4) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organizations, sets the policies for soliciting
     contributions or making expenditures of committee funds.

A sponsored committee (this includes most political action committees), must include the name of its sponsor in the name of
the committee. If the sponsored committee has more than one sponsor and the sponsors are members of an industry or other
identifiable group, the name of the committee must include a term identifying that industry or group.

VI    IF PRIMARILY FORMED TO SUPPORT OR OPPOSE SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR MEASURES, LIST
      SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR MEASURES SUPPORTED OR OPPOSED:

A committee is "primarily formed" when its principal activity is raising or spending money to support or oppose a specific
candidate or measure or a group of specific candidates all being voted upon in the same jurisdiction on the same date. This
type of committee is usually formed for a specific election and then terminates after the election when all debts have been
paid and there are no surplus funds.

VII   COMMITTEE'S PRINCIPAL ACTIVITY IF NOT SUPPORTING OR OPPOSING SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR
      MEASURES:

A committee which does not support or oppose specific candidates or measures as its primary activity must provide a brief
description of its political activities such as whether or not it supports candidates or measures which share a common political
affiliation.

VIII  YOU MUST SPECIFY WHAT DISPOSITION WILL BE MADE OF SURPLUS FUNDS IN THE EVENT OF
      TERMINATION

A committee must specify what use will be made of any surplus funds. Elections Code Section 12400, et seq. prohibits
personal use of campaign funds and specifies permissible uses of surplus campaign funds. Elections Code Section 29795
specifies permissible uses of funds received by state ballot measure committees. The Fair Political Practices Commission has no
authority to interpret the Elections Code. Contact the enforcement official for your jurisdiction if you have any questions.

VERIFICATION:

The statement must be signed and verified by the committee treasurer. If this committee is controlled by a candidate,
officeholder or state ballot measure proponent, the candidate, officeholder or proponent must also sign and verify the
statement. If a ballot measure committee is controlled by two or three candidates or officeholders, each candidate must sign
the Statement of Organization. If more than three candidates or officeholders control a ballot measure committee, one of
the candidates or officeholders may sign the Statement of Organization on behalf of all controlling candidates or
officeholders.

CHAMBERS OF

The Superior Court

800 SOUTH VICTORIA AVENUE
VENTURA, CA 93009
(805) 654-2915

Post Office Box 1467

This is intended to amend the original Statement of Organization
to change the specification of disposition of surplus funds upon
termination from "return to contributors" to "return to
contributors including repayment of loans to committee".

Thank you for your cooperation in this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
FREDERICK A. JONES
Judge of the Superior Court

For information required to be provided to you pursuant to the Information Practices Act of 1977, see "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure
Provisions of the Political Reform Act."

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING COVER PAGE
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION
FORM 410

NAME AND ADDRESS OF COMMITTEE:

Provide the full name of the committee. A committee may use only one name. If this committee is
controlled by an individual, entity or other person, the name of the individual, entity or other person must be
included in the name of the committee. For example, when a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state
ballot measure controls a committee, his or her name must be included in the name of the committee.

There are special rules for "sponsored committees," see Part V.

In the case of a committee supporting or opposing a ballot measure as its principal activity, all
references to the committee required by the Political Reform Act must include the statement,
"Committee For Proposition ________," if supporting a measure, or "Committee Against Proposition
________," if opposing a measure.

The street address of the committee must be reported. A post office box number is NOT acceptable.

The mailing address of the committee must be reported if it is different from the committee's street
address. A post office box number IS acceptable for the mailing address.

DATE QUALIFIED AS A COMMITTEE:

Date qualified is the date the committee received contributions totaling $1,000 or more during a
calendar year. If a Form 410 is filed prior to receipt of $1,000 in contributions, enter "Not Yet
Qualified" in the "Date Qualified as Committee" space.

I. TREASURER AND OTHER PRINCIPAL OFFICERS:

The treasurer's name, home or business address, a mailing address, if different than home or business
address, and a phone number where the treasurer can be reached during business hours must be
listed.

If the committee has "principal officers" other than the treasurer, such as a president, secretary or
chairperson, they must be identified.

II. IS THIS A BROAD BASED POLITICAL COMMITTEE?

To qualify as a "broad based committee," the committee may not be controlled by a candidate or
officeholder, the committee must have been in existence for more than six months, must have received
contributions from 100 or more persons at any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year (July 1
- June 30) and the previous two fiscal years, and must have made contributions to five or more candidates at
any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year and the previous two fiscal years.

Enter the date on or before which the committee qualified as a broad based committee. If the committee
qualified as a broad based committee on or before January 1, 1989, enter "On or before January 1, 1989."

III. IS THIS COMMITTEE CONTROLLED BY A CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR PROPONENT
OF A STATE BALLOT MEASURE?

A committee is controlled by a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state ballot measure if the
candidate, officeholder or proponent, their agent or any other committee they control, has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee.

Because of new provisions contained in Proposition 73 which was passed by the voters on June 7, 1988,
candidates and officeholders may not jointly control committees formed to support their own candidacies, or
committees formed to support or oppose other candidates. Candidates and officeholders may jointly control
a committee whose only activity is supporting or opposing a ballot measure.

If the letter is appropriate for confirmation without further
embedding.first_500_words: April 14, 1989 Honorable Frederick A. Jones Judge of the Superior Court 800 South Victoria Avenue Ventura, CA 93009 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-89-169 Dear Judge Jones: This will confirm our telephone conversation of February 22, 1989, in which we discussed the use of your accumulated campaign funds in light of the decision in California Common Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles County Superior Court Case No. C709383.). Since those funds were acquired prior to January 1, 1989, they would be restricted funds to be used for any lawful purpose other than to support or oppose a candidate. (Government Code Section 85306.) Thus, even though these funds were composed of contributions within statutory limits and deposited in a campaign bank account for a future campaign, the funds could not be used for future campaign expenditures unless the California Common Cause decision were reversed on appeal. I sent you a copy of Section 18536.2 of the California Code of Regulations which sets forth appropriate uses of restricted funds. As I explained to you, the California Common Cause case is still subject to appeal. We have been advising that no action be taken with respect to the disposition of restricted funds pending the appeal. If the funds now are placed in a restricted account, that election probably could not be reversed in the event that the California Common Cause decision were reversed on appeal. Notwithstanding this advice, you have decided not to await the outcome of the California Common Cause decision. You would prefer to close your campaign bank account and use the accumulated funds to repay campaign loans owed by your 1978 Municipal Court election committee. You have outlined the steps that you believe would be appropriate to accomplish 428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322- Honorable Frederick A. Jones April 14, 1989 Page 2 this result in your letter of March 14, 1989 (copy enclosed.) We have reviewed the proposed sequence and agree that it would be an acceptable way to proceed. If you have any questions about this letter, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901. Sincerely, Diane M. Griffiths General Counsel [signature] By: Margaret W. Ellison Counsel, Legal Division DMG:MWE:aa Enclosure CHAMBERS OF The Superior Court FREDERICK A. JONES HALL OF JUSTICE 800 SOUTH VICTORIA AVENUE VENTURA, CA 93009 (805) 654-2915 March 14, 1989 Ms. Margaret Ellison Staff Attorney Fair Political Practices Commission Post Office Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804-0807 Dear Ms. Ellison: Thank you for your recent advice regarding my apparently somewhat unique Proposition 73 predicament. I have reviewed the materials you provided and do now agree that, given California Common Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles Superior Court No. C709383), the election campaign money I accumulated while a judge of the Municipal Court may not continue to be held or later spent to promote my election as judge of the Superior Court. From among the available courses we discussed, I have decided to use the funds
